article.title: EU Council and Parliament Strike Provisional AI Act Omnibus Deal, Extending High-Risk Deadlines and Banning AI-Generated CSAM
article.summary: Negotiators reached a provisional agreement at 4:30 a.m. on May 7, trading 16-month deadline extensions for a new prohibition on AI-generated non-consensual imagery.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Negotiators for the European Council and the European Parliament reached a provisional agreement in the early hours of May 7, 2026, on the first set of amendments to the EU AI Act since the regulation was adopted in June 2024. The deal — concluded at 4:30 a.m. after overnight negotiations, [according to TechPolicy.Press](https://www.techpolicy.press/what-the-eu-ai-omnibus-deal-changes-for-the-ai-act-and-what-lies-ahead/) — forms part of Omnibus VII, one of ten simplification packages the EU has tabled since 2025 as part of its broader competitiveness agenda. The agreement extends the most significant compliance deadlines by up to 16 months while adding a new prohibition on AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery that was not in the Commission's original proposal.

The provisional deal must still be formally adopted by both institutions — a vote expected before August 2, 2026, when several current AI Act provisions would otherwise become enforceable — but its contents are now fixed barring extraordinary procedural reversal.

## What Was Agreed

### Timeline Extensions for High-Risk AI Systems

The most commercially significant element of the deal is a pair of deadline extensions for AI systems classified as high-risk under the AI Act's Annex III and Annex I frameworks.

Standalone high-risk AI systems — covering applications in employment, education, healthcare, and access to essential services — will now face compliance obligations from **December 2, 2027**, a 16-month extension from the original August 2, 2026 deadline, [as confirmed by Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/). High-risk AI systems embedded in safety-regulated products — such as lifts, toys, and medical devices — receive an extension to **August 2, 2028**, one year beyond the August 2, 2027 deadline previously in force, [according to The Lens](https://thelens.slaughterandmay.com/post/102ms2v/eu-agree-simplification-of-ai-rules).

A separate transparency deadline covering AI-generated content — specifically the watermarking and disclosure requirements under Article 50(2) — was extended by four months to **December 2, 2026**, with the grace period applying only to systems already placed on the market before August 2, 2026, [per Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/). The deadline for member states to establish national AI regulatory sandboxes was also extended by one year to **August 2, 2027**, and the deal establishes a new EU-level regulatory sandbox as an alternative, [as noted by Dastra](https://www.dastra.eu/en/blog/simpler-safer-stricter-where-it-counts-inside-the-eu-ai-omnibus-deal/60025).

### New Prohibition on Non-Consensual Intimate Imagery and CSAM

The agreement adds a prohibition not included in the Commission's original omnibus proposal: a ban on AI systems that generate non-consensual intimate imagery and child sexual abuse material (CSAM), effective **December 2, 2026**. The prohibition was added by Parliament negotiators and covers both providers and deployers, [as reported by Lewis Silkin](https://www.lewissilkin.com/insights/2026/05/07/the-council-and-parliament-agree-to-slim-down-and-delay-parts-of-the-eu-ai-act-102ms0v). Under the agreed text, providers are liable when generation of such content is the intended purpose or a reasonably foreseeable outcome without adequate safety measures in place, while deployers face liability only for intentional misuse — accidental generation is excluded from deployer liability, [according to Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/).

Penalties for violations of this prohibition can reach **€35 million or 7% of annual worldwide turnover**, whichever is higher, placing it in the same enforcement tier as the AI Act's other absolute prohibitions, [per Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/). TechPolicy.Press noted the provision gained momentum following the widely reported wave of sexual deepfakes generated through AI tools in the preceding months.

### Scope Adjustments and Simplification

The agreement narrows the definition of "safety component" for high-risk AI classification purposes. Under the revised text, AI systems that only assist users, optimize performance, or improve efficiency are excluded from high-risk classification unless a failure of the system could directly endanger health or safety, [according to The Lens](https://thelens.slaughterandmay.com/post/102ms2v/eu-agree-simplification-of-ai-rules). This change is expected to reduce the number of systems that must undergo full high-risk compliance procedures.

The machinery sector receives the broadest carve-out: AI systems regulated under the EU Machinery Regulation are exempted from the AI Act's direct high-risk obligations, with the Commission granted delegated act authority to introduce AI-specific health and safety requirements through the Machinery Regulation instead, [as reported by Lewis Silkin](https://www.lewissilkin.com/insights/2026/05/07/the-council-and-parliament-agree-to-slim-down-and-delay-parts-of-the-eu-ai-act-102ms0v). TechPolicy.Press noted that German Chancellor Friedrich Merz personally lobbied for industrial AI exemptions during the negotiations, and the machinery carve-out reflects that pressure, though it stops short of a full exemption for industrial AI as a category.

SME-tier compliance privileges, including simplified documentation and preferential sandbox access, are extended to small mid-cap companies that fall just above the formal SME threshold, [per Dastra](https://www.dastra.eu/en/blog/simpler-safer-stricter-where-it-counts-inside-the-eu-ai-omnibus-deal/60025).

### Bias Detection and Sensitive Data

The agreement allows AI providers to use GDPR-special-category personal data for bias detection and mitigation purposes across all AI systems — not only high-risk ones — subject to strict conditions including a "strictly necessary" standard, technical reuse limitations, access controls, confidentiality obligations, no third-party data transfer, and deletion once bias correction is complete, [as detailed by Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/). Lewis Silkin noted that this provision reinstates a "strict necessity" standard that had been subject to debate during earlier drafts.

### AI Office Authority Expanded

The AI Office — the EU's central AI regulatory body — gains exclusive supervisory competence over AI systems based on general-purpose AI models where the same provider develops both the underlying model and the system, as well as over AI systems embedded in very large online platforms or search engines designated under the Digital Services Act, [according to The Lens](https://thelens.slaughterandmay.com/post/102ms2v/eu-agree-simplification-of-ai-rules). Exceptions apply for law enforcement, border management, judicial, and financial institution use cases, which remain under national competent authority oversight. The AI Office also gains pre-market assessment authority for high-risk systems, on-site inspection capability, and the ability to impose binding commitments, [per Inside Privacy](https://www.insideprivacy.com/artificial-intelligence/eu-ai-act-update-timeline-relief-targeted-simplification-and-new-prohibitions/).

## What This Means

Henna Virkkunen, the EU's Executive Vice-President for Tech Sovereignty, Security and Democracy, framed the deal in terms of dual citizen and business expectations: "Our businesses and citizens want two things from AI rules. They want to be able to innovate and feel safe," [as quoted by Dastra](https://www.dastra.eu/en/blog/simpler-safer-stricter-where-it-counts-inside-the-eu-ai-omnibus-deal/60025).

The agreement was steered through Parliament by co-rapporteurs Arba Kokalari and Michael McNamara, [according to TechPolicy.Press](https://www.techpolicy.press/what-the-eu-ai-omnibus-deal-changes-for-the-ai-act-and-what-lies-ahead/).

For businesses that had been preparing for August 2026 compliance, the agreement provides significant breathing room. The 16-month extension for Annex III systems in particular affects large swaths of the AI market, including hiring software, credit-scoring tools, and educational assessment systems — all of which fall within the high-risk categories that would have activated in August. The narrower safety component definition further reduces the pool of systems that require full compliance procedures.

For civil society and data protection advocates, the picture is mixed. The new prohibition on AI-generated CSAM and non-consensual intimate imagery represents a genuine rights-protective addition to the regulation, one that Parliament negotiators inserted against the Commission's more industry-friendly starting position. But the deadline extensions and scope reductions continue a pattern of retreat from the AI Act's original ambition that The Machine Herald has tracked since the Commission first tabled the omnibus package and [civil society groups raised alarms about the direction of travel](/article/2026-02/25-eu-privacy-regulators-and-civil-society-push-back-as-digital-omnibus-moves-to-weaken-gdpr-and-delay-ai-act-protections) in February, the [Council and Parliament adopted their respective mandates in April](/article/2026-04/03-eu-council-and-parliament-advance-ai-act-overhaul-delaying-high-risk-rules-as-civil-society-warns-of-rights-rollback), and [enforcement readiness gaps emerged](/article/2026-04/23-only-8-of-27-eu-member-states-are-ready-to-enforce-the-ai-act-as-the-august-deadline-nears) just weeks before the original August deadline.

## What We Don't Know

The final formal text of the amendments has not been published; the provisional agreement is a political document, and the precise legislative language will emerge during formal adoption proceedings expected in June and July 2026. TechPolicy.Press flagged that a separate Data Omnibus package — which would narrow the definition of personal data under GDPR and potentially classify AI training as a legitimate data processing interest — poses longer-term implications that are still being worked through a parallel track.

The practical enforcement implications of the deadline extensions remain unclear for organizations that had already invested in August 2026 compliance preparations. Whether the extensions create an opportunity to defer or merely redistribute compliance work toward the new deadlines will depend on guidance from national competent authorities and the AI Office, neither of which had issued updated guidance at time of writing.
